content:
# Jude — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** Jude (Greek: Iouda) | **Chapters:** 1

## At a Glance
- **Author:** "Jude, a servant of Jesus Christ and a brother of James" (v. 1). If James is the brother of Jesus, Jude is also Jesus' brother (Mark 6:3 lists "James and Joses and Judas and Simon" as Jesus' brothers). Whether the letter is by this Jude or a later author writing in his name is debated.
- **Date:** c. 65–90 CE. The letter warns against false teachers who have "secretly slipped in" (v. 4), suggesting an established community facing internal disruption.
- **Audience:** General — "to those who have been called" (v. 1).
- **Genre:** Letter — brief, polemical, dense with allusion.
- **Timeline:** c. 65–90 CE.
- **In One Sentence:** Jude is a short, fiery letter warning against false teachers — notable for quoting two non-canonical Jewish texts (1 Enoch and the Assumption of Moses) as authoritative.

## The World Behind the Text
The false teachers are described in vivid, metaphorical language: "wild waves of the sea, foaming up their shame; wandering stars, for whom blackest darkness has been reserved forever" (v. 13). Their specific errors include rejecting authority (v. 8), reviling celestial beings (v. 8), and turning grace into license for immorality (v. 4). The exact identity of these opponents cannot be determined from the text.

## Why This Book Was Written
Jude's most distinctive feature is its use of non-canonical Jewish texts. Verse 9 — the archangel Michael disputing with the devil over Moses' body — draws on a tradition attributed to the Assumption/Testament of Moses (a Jewish text known from fragments and patristic citations). Verses 14–15 explicitly quote 1 Enoch 1:9: "Enoch, the seventh from Adam, prophesied about them: 'See, the Lord is coming with thousands upon thousands of his holy ones, to judge everyone.'"

The citation of 1 Enoch as prophecy raises questions about the author's view of that text's authority. 1 Enoch was widely read in Second Temple Judaism (fragments of all sections except the Book of Parables were found at Qumran) and was considered scripture by the Ethiopian Orthodox Church (which includes it in its biblical canon to this day).

## How to Read This Book
**vv. 1–4:** Greeting and occasion — "I felt compelled to write and urge you to contend for the faith" (v. 3). **vv. 5–16:** Three OT examples of judgment (Israel in the wilderness, fallen angels, Sodom and Gomorrah), plus the Michael-devil dispute and the Enoch quotation. **vv. 17–23:** Exhortation — remember the apostles' predictions, build yourselves up, show mercy. **vv. 24–25:** Doxology — "To him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you before his glorious presence without fault and with great joy" — one of the most celebrated doxologies in the NT.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
2 Peter chapter 2 borrows extensively from Jude — nearly the entire letter is incorporated into 2 Peter, with modifications. The most significant change: 2 Peter omits the 1 Enoch quotation and the Assumption of Moses reference, replacing them with canonical allusions. This editorial decision reveals different attitudes toward non-canonical texts within the NT itself.

The "fallen angels" reference (v. 6) — "angels who did not keep their positions of authority but abandoned their proper dwelling" — connects to Genesis 6:1–4 as interpreted in 1 Enoch 6–16 (the Watchers narrative). This interpretive tradition was widely shared in Second Temple Judaism.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **Jude quotes 1 Enoch as prophecy (vv. 14–15)** — this is the only explicit quotation of 1 Enoch in the NT. The Ethiopian Orthodox Church's inclusion of 1 Enoch in its canon is partly based on this citation.
- **The Michael-devil dispute (v. 9)** over Moses' body has no source in the Hebrew Bible. The tradition is attributed to the Assumption of Moses, a text known only from fragments and patristic references.
- **2 Peter's editorial treatment of Jude** — removing the non-canonical references while preserving the polemic — is a documented case of canonical editing within the NT.
- **"Wandering stars" (v. 13)** — planetēs asteres — the Greek word for "planet" (planetēs) means "wanderer." The false teachers are compared to planets whose erratic movement (from an earth-centered perspective) contrasts with the fixed stars.
- **P72 (3rd–4th century)** contains the complete text of Jude — the earliest manuscript. P78 (3rd–4th century) preserves a fragment.

## Timeline
- **c. 65–90 CE:** Composition
- **3rd–4th century CE:** P72, P78 — earliest manuscripts
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
